title: Klebsiella
body_html:
Background
Klebsiella pneumoniae is a Gram-negative, facultatively anaerobic, rod that is part of the normal human gastrointestinal tract flora. It can cause a number of infections in humans including pneumonia (typically following aspiration), urinary tract infections, bloodstream infections, wound or surgical site infections and meningitis. Klebsiella has a prominent polysaccharide capsule, which contributes significantly to its virulence and ability to evade host immune responses.

Epidemiology
Klebsiella species are well-recognised opportunistic pathogens. In the UK, Klebsiella infections are particularly associated with nosocomial (hospital-acquired) infections, especially in critical care settings such as intensive care units (ICUs). Ventilator-associated pneumonia and catheter-associated urinary tract infections are key clinical concerns.

Klebsiella is known for its role in outbreaks within healthcare environments and its increasing resistance to multiple antibiotics, including the production of extended-spectrum beta-lactamases (ESBLs) and carbapenemases. This makes its management particularly challenging.

Clinical features of Klebsiella pneumonia
  • More common in alcoholic and diabetics
  • May occur following aspiration
  • 'Red-currant jelly' sputum
  • Often affects upper lobes
  • Cavitating pneumonia in up to 50% of cases

Prognosis
Klebsiella infections can range from mild UTIs to fulminant pneumonia and sepsis.

Klebsiella pneumoniae pneumonia, particularly when ventilator-associated or in patients with comorbidities, is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Klebsiella pneumonia commonly causes lung abscess formation and empyema with a high mortality of 20-50%

A human neutrophil interacting with Klebsiella pneumoniae (pink), a multidrug-resistant bacterium that causes severe hospital infections. Credit: NIAID

Medical management
The treatment of Klebsiella infections must be guided by antibiotic susceptibility testing, due to widespread resistance.
